question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: Methods: We conducted an individual patient data analysis of PHNSCC. Two authors independently searched PubMed, google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies using following search words: Pediatric Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of 20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC till June 1, 2016 published in English language.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: Two authors (BP and SM) independently searched PubMed, google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies using following search words: pediatric Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of 20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC till June 1, 2016 published in English language.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: References from the primary search result also were manually searched for potentially eligible studies.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: Two authors independently searched PubMed, google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies using following search words: Pediatric Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of 20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC till June 1, 2016 published in English language.

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: Two authors independently searched PubMed, google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies using following search words: Pediatric Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of 20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC till June 1, 2016 published in English language.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: Two independent authors (BP) and(SM) selected the eligible studies. Any disagreement between two authors was solved by discussing with a third author (GKR). Study selection has been depicted in Fig. 1 PRISMA flow diagram.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers: Two authors independently (BP, SM) extracted all data from the eligible trials

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers: Total of 217 patients of PHNSCC were found in the literature. Individual patient data was available for 107 patients from 54 publications and statistical analysis was performed on 107 patients. Median age of the cohort was 15 years (Range: 0e20 years).22(20.6%) patients were below 10 years and 85(79.4%) were between 11 and 20 years of age. Sex ratio was skewed in favor of males with ratio of 1.7:1 (Male: 66, female: 38). Oral cavity tumors were, commonest 75 (70%) followed by laryngeal neoplasms 16(15%) [Fig. 2]. Among oral cavity tumors, tongue 56(74.67%) was involved most commonly followed by gingiva 10(13.3%), retromolar trigone1(1.3%), Upper lip 1(1.3%), Lower lip 1(1.3%), buccal mucosa 4(5.3%), hard palate 1(1.3%), floor of mouth 1(1.3%) [Fig. 3]. 11(10.2%) patients had DNA repair defect, fanconis anemia and 9(8.4%) patients had other risk factors like smoking(3), tobacco chewing(1), HPV infection , xerodera pigmenstosa(2),retinoblastoma survior(1) and sharp teeth(1). Surgery alone was used in 42(39.3%), radiotherapy alone in 15(14%), surgery followed by adjuvant radiotherapy in 24(22.4%), concurrent radiotherapy and chemotherapy in 15(14%), trimodality therapy 3(2.1%). Treatment data was not available for 8 (7.5%) [Table 2]. Fig. 4 depicts the various treatment modalities used.Out of 15 patients treated with radiation only external beam radiotherapy was used in 11 patients and brachytherapy was used in 4 patients. Interstitial brachytherapy with radon seeds and external beam was used in 2 patients, 10 g of potassium Iodide was used in 1 patient, and interstitial brachytherapy with neck dissection was used in 1 patient. Surgery with or without radiotherapy was used in 66 patients. Out of these 66 patients surgery for the primary site alone was done in 31 patients and surgery with neck dissection was done in 20 patients. Among 20 neck dissections 8 were selective neck dissections, 9 were modified neck dissection, 1 radical neck dissection and in 2 patient's neck dissection type were not specified. Brachytherapy for primary with neck dissection for nodes was used in 1, brachytherapy followed by wide local excision and neck dissection was done in 2 patients. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by wide local excision and modified neck dissection followed by post-operative radiotherapy was used in 1 patient. Surgery followed by post-operative radiotherapy was used in 11 patients. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy was used in 4 patients among which 1 patient had docetaxel, cisplatin and 5 Fluorouracil and 1 patient received paclitaxel and carboplatin and data was NA in 2 patients. Brachytherapy with chemotherapy was used in 1 patient and WLE with adjuvant chemo-radiation was used in 1 patient. Palliative chemotherapy alone was used in 6 patients with Hydroxyurea-1, Methotrexate-1, Methotrexate with cyclophosphamide-1, Retinoic acid and 5 Flououracil-1, data not available-2 patients.
